question: Compare the systems eliminated in Table 1.3-2 with the passive alternatives provided by Ch.05 safety systems.

nuscale_ch01_0020.title: Safety Considerations
nuscale_ch01_0020.summary: The NuScale design enhances safety by maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel (CNV), which reduces corrosion, aids in leak detection, and limits combustible gases. Its passive safety systems and integral design eliminate the need for external power and large coolant loops, significantly lowering the risk of core damage and improving safety compared to traditional nuclear plants.

nuscale_ch01_0021.title: Engineered Safety Features and Emergency Systems
nuscale_ch01_0021.summary: The NuScale reactor uses a vacuum in the containment vessel (CNV) to reduce corrosion, improve leak detection, and limit combustible gases, while also eliminating the need for insulation and reducing debris. Its passive safety systems, including emergency core cooling and decay heat removal, operate without external power and eliminate large-break loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) risks, resulting in a lower core damage frequency and a smaller radioactive source term compared to conventional reactors.

nuscale_ch01_0558.title: DSRS 12.3-12.4, Rev 0: Radiation Protection Design FeaturesII.9 RG 8.15
nuscale_ch01_0558.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with various regulatory guides related to radiation protection design features, as outlined in DSRS 12.3-12.4, Rev 0. It details whether each guide is applicable, not applicable, conforms, or partially conforms, with references to supporting sections and tables for further information.

nuscale_ch01_0023.title: Containment Systems
nuscale_ch01_0023.summary: The NuScale containment vessel (CNV) is kept under vacuum during normal operations, which reduces moisture and corrosion, aids in leak detection, and limits oxygen for safety. In the event of an emergency, passive cooling rapidly reduces pressure and temperature, and the plant’s design—featuring passive safety systems and a small fuel inventory—results in improved safety and a lower core damage frequency compared to traditional reactors.

nuscale_ch01_0052.title: Comparison with Other Facilities
nuscale_ch01_0052.summary: This section compares the major design features and nominal parameters of the NuScale Power Plant with those of a typical pressurized water reactor (PWR). It highlights differences in aspects such as electrical output, core design, safety systems, and reactor components, using summary tables for direct comparison. The data provided are nominal values intended for reference only.

nuscale_ch01_0669.title: DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0: Decrease in Feedwater Temperature, Increase in Feedwater Flow, Increase in Steam Flow, and Inadvertent Opening of the Turbine Bypass System or Inadvertent Operation of the Decay Heat Removal SystemII.4 Specific CriterionInstrument Spans and Setpoints use RG 1.105
nuscale_ch01_0669.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for abnormal operational occurrences such as changes in feedwater temperature, flow, steam flow, and inadvertent system operations. It confirms that NuScale meets specific criteria for system pressure, critical heat flux, event classification, and instrument setpoints, aligning with NRC guidance and ensuring plant safety under these conditions. The report references compliance with relevant standards, including RG 1.105 and the Standard Review Plan.

nuscale_ch01_0051.title: Plant Features of Special Interest
nuscale_ch01_0051.summary: This section describes key plant features of special interest in the NuScale Power Plant, including the Central Utility Building (CUB) and Diesel Generator Buildings (DGBs), which house essential but nonsafety-related systems and backup generators. It also highlights human factors considerations, emphasizing fail-safe design, automation, digital controls, and workload management to minimize human error and meet regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0863.title: Table 1.9-8: Conformance with SECY-93-087, "Policy, Technical, and Licensing Issues Pertaining to Evolutionary and Advanced Light-Water Reactor Designs"
nuscale_ch01_0863.summary: Table 1.9-8 summarizes NuScale’s conformance with SECY-93-087 policy and technical issues for advanced light-water reactor designs. It lists each regulatory issue, NuScale’s conformance status (conforms, partially conforms, or not applicable), and provides brief comments and relevant FSAR sections. The table demonstrates NuScale’s alignment with most criteria, with partial conformance or non-applicability noted for specific issues.

nuscale_ch01_0024.title: Emergency Core Cooling System
nuscale_ch01_0024.summary: The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) in the NuScale design is a fully passive system that removes decay heat during a loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) using three independent reactor vent valves and two recirculation valves, all of which are closed during normal operation. During an emergency, the system vents steam into containment where it condenses, and recirculation valves return water to the core, with continued cooling achieved by heat conduction through the containment vessel wall to the reactor pool.

nuscale_ch01_0666.title: DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0: Decrease in Feedwater Temperature, Increase in Feedwater Flow, Increase in Steam Flow, and Inadvertent Opening of the Turbine Bypass System or Inadvertent Operation of the Decay Heat Removal SystemII.1 Specific CriterionSystem Pressure
nuscale_ch01_0666.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for abnormal operational occurrences such as changes in feedwater temperature, flow, steam flow, and inadvertent system operations. It confirms that NuScale meets requirements for system pressure, critical heat flux, event classification, and instrument setpoints, ensuring that such events do not escalate into more serious conditions and that all criteria align with regulatory standards.

nuscale_ch01_0063.title: Emergency Core Cooling System Valve Design Certification Application Demonstration Test
nuscale_ch01_0063.summary: This section describes the demonstration test program for the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) valves in the NuScale reactor design. The program aimed to verify the functional performance of ECCS valves, including their operation at reactor pressures and temperatures and in borated coolant, as well as to test unique design features like the inadvertent actuation block (IAB). Testing was conducted at a specialized facility using a representative valve assembly to ensure the system meets safety and operational requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0044.title: Chemical and Volume Control System
nuscale_ch01_0044.summary: The Chemical and Volume Control System section describes the NuScale reactor's closed-loop, two-phase natural circulation cooling system, which uses two independent decay heat removal trains, each capable of removing 100% of the decay heat load. In case of a steam generator tube failure, the system isolates the affected generator and continues cooling through the intact one, with heat ultimately transferred to a large, reinforced concrete pool (the ultimate heat sink) that passively absorbs and dissipates excess heat, even during a total loss of AC power.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain fuel integrity, minimize solid deposits, and ensure uniform boron concentration under all operating conditions.

edges.description: Section A and Section B are identical excerpts describing the benefits of maintaining a vacuum in the CNV, providing the same technical rationale relevant to both safety considerations and engineered safety features.

Section A describes specific safety benefits of maintaining a vacuum in the CNV, which supports the regulatory conformance claims in Section B regarding radiation protection design features by providing evidence of how the design addresses moisture, corrosion, and leakage detection relevant to radiation safety.
Section A and Section B are identical excerpts describing the benefits of maintaining a vacuum in the CNV, thus they provide the same information regarding containment system design features.

Section A describes the safety benefits of maintaining a vacuum in the CNV, which is a specific design feature that is compared in Section B against typical PWR plant designs in Table 1.3-1, connecting the unique NuScale system eliminations to the broader comparison of safety systems.

Section A describes the design features and operational benefits of maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel, which eliminates the need for certain engineered safety features, while Section B lists the degree of conformance of the plant's radiation protection design features with specific regulatory guides, indicating which requirements are met or only partially satisfied.

Section A and Section B are identical excerpts describing the benefits of maintaining a vacuum in the CNV, providing the same technical basis relevant to the elimination of certain systems and the adoption of passive alternatives.

Section B provides a comparative overview of systems (including those eliminated in Table 1.3-2) between NuScale and typical PWRs, while Section A describes specific design features (such as the CNV vacuum) that serve as passive alternatives to eliminated systems.

Section A lists regulatory guidance and conformance statements for radiation protection design features, some of which are partially met, while Section B describes the containment system's passive design features that may serve as alternatives to eliminated active systems referenced in Table 1.3-2, thus connecting the regulatory requirements with the design solutions.

Section A lists regulatory guidance and conformance statements for radiation protection design features, while Section B provides a comparative overview of NuScale's major design features (including safety systems) against typical PWRs, connecting them through the evaluation of how eliminated systems (from Table 1.3-2) are addressed by passive alternatives described in Ch.05.

Section B provides a comparative context for the systems described in Section A by listing major NuScale design features (such as the containment vacuum) alongside those of typical PWRs, enabling analysis of which systems are eliminated and what passive alternatives are implemented.

Section A lists specific system events and criteria for system pressure and instrument setpoints, while Section B describes passive design features (vacuum in the CNV) that provide alternative safety benefits to those systems eliminated in Table 1.3-2, connecting them as passive alternatives to active systems.

Section A lists specific transient events and their regulatory criteria, while Section B describes passive engineered safety features (such as the CNV vacuum) that serve as alternatives to active systems eliminated in Table 1.3-2, connecting them through the context of system replacement and safety function.

Section A discusses the conformance of specific system responses to operational transients (such as feedwater and steam flow changes) with regulatory criteria, while Section B addresses the conformance of radiation protection design features with their respective regulatory guides; both sections are connected through their documentation of how different safety-related systems (including those potentially eliminated or replaced by passive alternatives) meet or partially meet regulatory requirements.

Section A lists nonsafety-related utility systems housed in the Central Utility Building that are eliminated from safety functions, while Section B describes passive safety features (vacuum in the CNV) that provide alternative safety benefits, connecting the elimination of active systems in A with the passive alternatives in B.

Section A lists nonsafety-related utility systems housed in the Central Utility Building, some of which (e.g., instrument air, service air) are candidates for elimination and comparison with the passive safety features described in Section B, which details how the containment vessel's vacuum design provides safety functions without reliance on such active systems.

Section A lists nonsafety-related utility systems housed in the Central Utility Building, some of which may be among those eliminated in Table 1.3-2, while Section B details the degree of conformance of radiation protection design features with regulatory guidance, providing context for evaluating whether passive alternatives in Ch.05 meet or exceed these requirements.

Section A documents the NuScale design's conformance with regulatory criteria, while Section B provides specific safety features (such as maintaining a vacuum in the CNV) that support or exemplify how the design meets those criteria.

Section A documents regulatory conformance for design features, while Section B describes specific passive safety system characteristics that support the elimination of certain active systems referenced in Table 1.3-2, thus providing evidence that the passive alternatives satisfy regulatory expectations.

Section A documents the NuScale design's conformance with high-level regulatory policy issues (such as source term and ATWS), while Section B details the design's partial or non-applicability conformance with specific radiation protection regulatory guides, both providing evidence of how the design meets or addresses different regulatory requirements.

Section A describes the passive Emergency Core Cooling System's operation, which relies on the containment vessel (CNV) environment described in Section B, where maintaining a vacuum in the CNV supports ECCS effectiveness by minimizing moisture and facilitating heat removal.

Section A describes the passive Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) as a means of decay heat removal, while Section B outlines the containment vessel (CNV) vacuum conditions that support the reliability and effectiveness of passive safety features like the ECCS, connecting them as complementary passive safety strategies.

Section A lists specific system events and criteria related to system pressure that are addressed by regulatory requirements, while Section B describes passive design features (such as maintaining a vacuum in the CNV) that serve as alternatives to active systems eliminated in Table 1.3-2, connecting them as passive replacements for previously required active safety functions.

Section A lists specific transient events and their regulatory criteria, while Section B describes passive safety features (such as the CNV vacuum) that function as alternatives to active systems potentially eliminated as referenced in Table 1.3-2, connecting them through the context of system replacement and safety function.

Section A addresses system pressure criteria for certain transient events, while Section B outlines radiation protection design features, with both sections referencing conformance to regulatory guidance in the NuScale FSAR, thus connecting through their mutual evaluation of system design compliance with regulatory requirements.

Section A describes the design and testing of ECCS valves, which are part of the systems considered for elimination or replacement in Table 1.3-2, while Section B outlines passive safety features (such as maintaining a vacuum in the CNV) that serve as alternatives to active systems like ECCS valves.

Section A describes the design and testing of ECCS valves, which are part of the systems potentially eliminated in Table 1.3-2, while Section B outlines the passive safety features (such as the CNV vacuum) that serve as alternatives to active systems like those in Section A.

Section A describes the design and testing of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) valves, while Section B outlines the conformance of the NuScale design with various radiation protection regulatory guides, connecting them through the regulatory requirements that ECCS valve design must satisfy to ensure radiation protection and safety compliance.

Section A lists specific safety analysis events and criteria for system performance, while Section B describes plant utility systems, some of which (e.g., instrument air, chiller equipment) may be among those eliminated in Table 1.3-2 and thus relevant for comparison with passive alternatives in Ch.05.

Section A lists specific transient events and their regulatory criteria, while Section B documents how the overall design—including the elimination of certain systems—conforms to higher-level policy and technical requirements, thus Section B provides the regulatory context and justification for the system eliminations and passive alternatives discussed in Section A.

Section A lists specific events and criteria related to system performance and regulatory conformance, while Section B describes the passive Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) that serves as an alternative means of decay heat removal, connecting them through the replacement of eliminated active systems with passive safety features.

Section A references the same regulatory criteria and specific system events as Section B, indicating that A is citing or cross-referencing B to establish conformance with system pressure requirements.

Section A outlines the regulatory criteria and system eliminations for certain feedwater and steam events, while Section B provides test results and design details for the passive Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) valves that serve as alternatives to the eliminated systems referenced in Section A.

Section A lists nonsafety-related utility systems housed in the Central Utility Building, some of which may be among those eliminated in Table 1.3-2, while Section B documents regulatory conformance for safety-related systems, providing a basis to compare eliminated systems with passive alternatives in Ch.05.

Section A lists nonsafety-related utility systems housed in the Central Utility Building, some of which (e.g., instrument air system) are eliminated in favor of the passive Emergency Core Cooling System described in Section B, which provides safety functions without reliance on such active systems.

Section A lists nonsafety-related utility systems housed in the Central Utility Building, some of which are eliminated from safety functions, while Section B outlines regulatory criteria for system pressure responses to certain transients, connecting them by the need to ensure that eliminated systems do not compromise conformance with these regulatory requirements.

Section A lists nonsafety-related utility systems housed in the Central Utility Building, some of which are eliminated from safety functions, while Section B describes the emergency core cooling system valves that serve as passive safety alternatives, connecting the eliminated systems in Table 1.3-2 to their passive replacements in Ch.05.

Section A references regulatory conformance criteria that include the use of passive safety features, while Section B describes the Emergency Core Cooling System as a passive safety system, thus Section B provides a specific example that supports the general regulatory conformance discussed in Section A.

Section A documents the NuScale design's conformance with broad regulatory policy issues (including system eliminations) while Section B details the system's compliance with specific safety analysis criteria for operational transients, both referencing the Final Safety Analysis Report to demonstrate regulatory compliance.

Section A documents the NuScale design's conformance with regulatory criteria, including the use of passive safety features as alternatives to eliminated systems, while Section B provides test results demonstrating the operability and effectiveness of one such passive system (the ECCS valves), thereby supporting the claims of regulatory conformance in Section A.

Section A describes the passive Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) design, while Section B lists regulatory criteria (DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4) that the plant's systems must conform to, connecting them through the requirement that the ECCS must satisfy these regulatory accident scenarios.

Section A describes the functional design and passive operation of the ECCS, while Section B provides test results confirming that the ECCS valves (as described in Section A) meet operational requirements, thus connecting the design description to its demonstrated performance.

Section A lists specific systems and events that are eliminated or not required per regulatory criteria, while Section B describes the passive emergency core cooling system (ECCS) valves that serve as alternatives to active systems, thus Section B provides passive alternatives to the systems eliminated in Section A.

Section A describes the passive decay heat removal system that replaces the eliminated Chemical and Volume Control System, while Section B outlines safety benefits (such as leak detection and reduced corrosion) provided by maintaining a vacuum in the containment, which is enabled by the elimination of active systems like the Chemical and Volume Control System.

Section A describes the passive decay heat removal capability that replaces active systems like the Chemical and Volume Control System, while Section B outlines containment features that support passive safety by maintaining a vacuum, both contributing to the elimination of traditional active safety systems as compared in Table 1.3-2.

Section A describes the passive safety system (decay heat removal) that replaces active systems like the Chemical and Volume Control System, while Section B lists regulatory guidance and conformance related to radiation protection, which are requirements that the passive alternatives in Section A must satisfy.

Section A describes the design choice to eliminate reactor coolant pumps in favor of natural circulation, while Section B outlines safety benefits (such as reduced corrosion and improved leak detection) that result from maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel, both of which are passive features replacing active systems eliminated as listed in Table 1.3-2.

Section A describes the passive design features of the reactor coolant system that eliminate the need for active components, while Section B outlines the benefits of maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel, both of which serve as passive alternatives to systems eliminated as listed in Table 1.3-2.

Section A describes the passive design features of the NuScale reactor coolant system, which eliminates certain active systems, while Section B lists regulatory conformance for radiation protection features, some of which are affected by the elimination of those active systems.

Section A describes the elimination of the Chemical and Volume Control System in favor of a closed-loop, passive decay heat removal system, while Section B outlines the design basis of the reactor coolant system that relies on natural circulation and excludes active components, showing that the passive alternatives in Ch.05 replace the eliminated systems listed in Table 1.3-2.
